tasks.title: Implement Lexer for Go Compatibility
tasks.description: Create a lexer that tokenizes Go source code with Goo extensions
tasks.details:
Implement a lexer that can tokenize all valid Go syntax plus Goo extensions including:
- Standard Go tokens (keywords, operators, literals)
- Goo-specific tokens: '!' for error unions, '?' for nullable types
- Channel pattern keywords (pub, sub, req, rep)
- 'comptime' keyword
- Ownership qualifiers

The lexer should:
1. Read source code from .goo files
2. Produce a token stream with position information
3. Handle all Go escape sequences
4. Support Unicode identifiers

Implementation approach:
```go
type TokenType int

const (
    // Standard Go tokens
    TOKEN_IDENT TokenType = iota
    TOKEN_INT
    TOKEN_FLOAT
    TOKEN_STRING
    // ... other Go tokens
    
    // Goo extensions
    TOKEN_BANG        // !
    TOKEN_QUESTION    // ?
    TOKEN_COMPTIME    // comptime
    TOKEN_PUB         // pub (channel pattern)
    TOKEN_SUB         // sub (channel pattern)
    TOKEN_REQ         // req (channel pattern)
    TOKEN_REP         // rep (channel pattern)
    // ... ownership tokens
)

type Token struct {
    Type    TokenType
    Literal string
    Pos     Position
}

type Lexer struct {
    input        string
    position     int
    readPosition int
    ch           byte
    line         int
    column       int
}

func NewLexer(input string) *Lexer {...}
func (l *Lexer) NextToken() Token {...}
```
tasks.testStrategy:
1. Unit test each token type recognition
2. Test with valid Go programs to ensure all syntax is recognized
3. Test with Goo extensions to verify new token types
4. Test error cases with invalid syntax
5. Test Unicode handling
6. Benchmark performance with large files
7. Compare output with Go's standard lexer for compatibility validation

tasks.title: Develop Parser for AST Generation
tasks.description: Create a parser that builds an Abstract Syntax Tree from the token stream
tasks.details:
Implement a recursive descent parser that converts the token stream from the lexer into an Abstract Syntax Tree (AST). The parser should:

1. Support all Go syntax (packages, imports, declarations, statements, expressions)
2. Add support for Goo extensions (error unions, nullable types, channel patterns)
3. Build a complete AST with position information for error reporting
4. Handle operator precedence correctly

Core AST structures:
```go
type Node interface {
    Pos() Position
    Type() Type  // Will be filled during type checking
}

type Program struct {
    Package     string
    Imports     []ImportSpec
    Declarations []Declaration
}

type FuncDecl struct {
    Name       string
    Params     []Param
    ReturnType Type  // Can be error union with !
    Body       []Stmt
    Attributes []Attr  // comptime, unsafe, etc.
}

type ChannelType struct {
    Pattern  ChannelPattern  // basic, pub, sub, req, rep
    ElemType Type
    Endpoint string  // Optional network endpoint
}
```

Implementation approach:
1. Create a Parser struct that takes a Lexer
2. Implement recursive descent parsing for each grammar rule
3. Use precedence climbing for expression parsing
4. Build appropriate AST nodes for each construct
5. Track and attach source positions to nodes
6. Add special handling for Goo extensions
tasks.testStrategy:
1. Unit test parsing of individual constructs (functions, types, expressions)
2. Test with complete Go programs to verify full syntax support
3. Test Goo extensions specifically
4. Test error recovery and reporting
5. Verify AST structure matches expected output
6. Test with malformed input to ensure robust error handling
7. Compare parsed structure with Go's standard parser for compatibility

tasks.title: Implement Type System and Type Checker
tasks.description: Create the type system with Goo extensions and implement type checking for the AST
tasks.details:
Implement the type system and type checker that enforces type safety, including Goo's ownership tracking, nullable types, and error unions.

Type System Components:
```go
type Type interface {
    String() string
    Size() int
    Align() int
}

type OwnershipKind int
const (
    Owned OwnershipKind = iota
    Borrowed
    Shared
)

type QualifiedType struct {
    Base      Type
    Nullable  bool
    Ownership OwnershipKind
}

type ErrorUnionType struct {
    ValueType Type
    ErrorType Type  // Usually a standard error type
}

type ChannelType struct {
    Pattern  ChannelPattern
    ElemType Type
}
```

Type Checker Implementation:
1. Create a TypeChecker struct that traverses the AST
2. Implement type inference for expressions
3. Verify type compatibility in assignments and function calls
4. Check ownership rules (simplified from Rust)
5. Enforce null safety with nullable types
6. Validate error union handling
7. Verify channel pattern usage

Ownership Checking:
- Track variable ownership through scopes
- Detect use-after-move errors
- Implement borrowing rules
- Generate clear error messages for ownership violations

Null Safety:
- Ensure nullable types are checked before use
- Track null state through control flow
- Prevent null pointer dereferences
tasks.testStrategy:
1. Unit test type checking for basic expressions
2. Test type inference in various contexts
3. Test ownership tracking with move semantics
4. Test nullable type safety enforcement
5. Test error union handling and propagation
6. Verify channel type compatibility
7. Test with intentional type errors to verify error reporting
8. Benchmark performance with large codebases

tasks.title: Develop LLVM IR Code Generator
tasks.description: Create a code generator that translates the typed AST to LLVM IR
tasks.details:
Implement a code generator that translates the typed AST into LLVM IR for compilation to native code. The code generator should:

1. Set up LLVM module, context, and builder
2. Generate LLVM types from Goo types
3. Generate function declarations and definitions
4. Translate expressions to LLVM instructions
5. Implement control flow (if, for, switch)
6. Handle function calls and returns
7. Support error unions and nullable types

LLVM Integration:
```go
type CodeGenerator struct {
    context  llvm.Context
    module   llvm.Module
    builder  llvm.Builder
    function llvm.Value
    typeMap  map[Type]llvm.Type
    valueMap map[string]llvm.Value
}

func NewCodeGenerator(moduleName string) *CodeGenerator {...}
func (g *CodeGenerator) GenerateProgram(program *Program) error {...}
func (g *CodeGenerator) GenerateFunction(funcDecl *FuncDecl) (llvm.Value, error) {...}
func (g *CodeGenerator) GenerateExpression(expr Expr) (llvm.Value, error) {...}
```

Special Handling:
1. Error unions: Generate code for error checking and propagation
2. Nullable types: Generate null checks before dereference
3. Ownership: Generate move/copy operations based on ownership
4. Channel operations: Generate runtime calls for channel operations

Optimization:
- Apply LLVM optimization passes
- Implement inlining for small functions
- Use LLVM's constant folding

tasks.title: Implement Null Safety System
tasks.description: Implement nullable types with ? syntax and safe unwrapping
tasks.details:
Implement the null safety system with ? nullable types and safe unwrapping. This includes:

1. Extend the type system to support nullable types (?T)
2. Implement null checking before dereference
3. Add if-let syntax for safe unwrapping
4. Generate appropriate LLVM IR for null checks

Language Syntax:
```go
// Nullable variable declaration
var name: ?string = nil

// Safe unwrapping with if-let
if let actualName = name {
    // actualName is non-nullable string here
    fmt.Println("Hello, " + actualName)
} else {
    fmt.Println("Hello, anonymous")
}

// Forced unwrapping (will panic if nil)
fmt.Println("Hello, " + name!)
```

Implementation Details:
1. Modify the parser to recognize ? type syntax
2. Extend the type checker to handle nullable types
3. Implement if-let syntax in the AST
4. Generate LLVM IR for null checking
5. Create runtime support for null checking
6. Implement panic for forced unwrapping of nil values

The null safety system should prevent null pointer dereferences at compile time when possible, and add runtime checks when needed.

tasks.title: Implement Data Parallel Processing
tasks.description: Add an OpenMP-like parallel programming model with parallel for loops, work distribution, and synchronization primitives to enable efficient data-parallel operations while maintaining memory safety.
tasks.details:
Implement a comprehensive data parallel processing system that builds on the existing goroutine infrastructure to provide OpenMP-like functionality:

1. Parallel For Loops:
   - Implement a `parallel for` construct that automatically distributes iterations across available threads
   - Support different scheduling strategies (static, dynamic, guided)
   - Allow specification of chunk sizes for work distribution
   ```go
   parallel for i := 0; i < len(array); i++ {
       // Work done in parallel
   }
   ```

2. Work Distribution Mechanisms:
   - Implement work-stealing queues for dynamic load balancing
   - Create thread pool management for efficient worker reuse
   - Support task chunking for granularity control

3. Shared Memory Synchronization:
   - Implement atomic operations (compare-and-swap, fetch-and-add)
   - Add mutex and read-write lock primitives
   - Provide memory fences/barriers for ordering operations
   ```go
   var counter atomic.Int
   counter.Add(1)
   ```

4. Reduction Operations:
   - Implement parallel reduction for common operations:
     - Sum, product, min, max
     - Custom user-defined reductions
   ```go
   sum := parallel reduce(array, 0, func(a, b int) int { return a + b })
   ```

5. Thread-Local Storage:
   - Implement thread-local variables for per-thread data
   - Provide efficient access patterns for thread-local data
   ```go
   threadLocal var counter int
   ```

6. Barrier Synchronization:
   - Add barrier primitives for synchronizing parallel sections
   - Support named barriers for complex synchronization patterns
   ```go
   barrier()  // All threads must reach this point before any proceed
   ```

7. Integration with Ownership System:
   - Extend the type system to track data access patterns in parallel regions
   - Implement compile-time checks for data race detection
   - Add runtime verification for dynamic parallel patterns
   - Ensure memory safety guarantees are maintained during parallel execution

8. Runtime Support:
   - Extend the runtime system to manage thread pools
   - Implement work distribution algorithms
   - Add performance monitoring and tuning capabilities

The implementation should focus on both performance and safety, ensuring that the parallel constructs integrate seamlessly with Goo's ownership system to prevent data races while maintaining high performance.

2. Race Detection:
   - Implement test cases with intentional race conditions to verify detection

3. Performance Testing:

tasks.title: Implement Pattern Matching
tasks.description: Add match expressions with exhaustiveness checking, pattern compilation, and guard conditions for safe and ergonomic control flow.
tasks.details:
Implement pattern matching as a first-class language feature that provides a more powerful and safer alternative to switch statements. The implementation should include:

1. AST Extensions:
   - Create new AST node types for match expressions, patterns, and guards
   - Define pattern types: literal patterns, variable bindings, destructuring patterns, type patterns, and wildcard patterns

2. Pattern Compilation:
   - Implement a pattern compiler that translates patterns into efficient runtime checks
   - Support nested patterns for complex data structures
   - Implement destructuring for structs, arrays, slices, and custom types

3. Exhaustiveness Checking:
   - Implement static analysis to verify all possible cases are handled
   - Generate compile-time errors for non-exhaustive matches
   - Consider type information to determine possible variants

4. Guard Conditions:
   - Support boolean expressions as additional filters on patterns
   - Implement scoping rules for variables bound in patterns and used in guards

5. Type Refinement:
   - Track type narrowing through pattern matching
   - Update the type environment for the scope of each match arm

Example syntax:
```go
match value {
  case 0:
    fmt.Println("Zero")
  case n if n < 0:
    fmt.Println("Negative:", n)
  case n if n > 0 && n < 10:
    fmt.Println("Small positive:", n)
  case n:
    fmt.Println("Large positive:", n)
}

match err {
  case nil:
    // Success case
  case io.EOF:
    // Specific error handling
  case e:
    // Default error case
}

match obj {
  case Person{Name: name, Age: age} if age > 18:
    fmt.Println(name, "is an adult")
  case Person{Name: name}:
    fmt.Println(name, "is a minor")
  case _:
    fmt.Println("Not a person")
}
```

Integration with the type system:
- Leverage the existing type system to determine possible variants for exhaustiveness checking
- Ensure pattern matching works with all Goo type extensions (nullable types, error unions)
- Implement special handling for error unions to encourage proper error handling

Performance considerations:
- Compile patterns to efficient decision trees or jump tables where possible
- Minimize runtime overhead for pattern evaluation
- Optimize common patterns (like enum matching) for better performance

   - Create unit tests for each pattern type (literals, variables, destructuring, etc.)

tasks.title: Implement Unsafe Blocks & Hardware Control
tasks.description: Add unsafe syntax and semantics to the language, including raw pointer operations, inline assembly support, FFI capabilities, and hardware control primitives for LughOS integration.
tasks.details:
This task involves implementing unsafe features that allow low-level system programming while maintaining the language's safety guarantees elsewhere:

1. Unsafe Block Syntax:
   - Implement `unsafe` keyword and block syntax
   - Create parser rules for unsafe blocks in the AST
   - Add type checking rules specific to unsafe contexts
   - Ensure unsafe operations are only allowed within unsafe blocks

2. Raw Pointer Operations:
   - Implement pointer type syntax (e.g., `*unsafe.Pointer`)
   - Add pointer arithmetic operations (+, -, [], etc.)
   - Implement pointer casting between different types
   - Support dereferencing operations with proper bounds checking options

3. Inline Assembly Support:
   - Create syntax for inline assembly blocks (e.g., `asm { ... }`)
   - Support different assembly dialects (AT&T, Intel)
   - Implement operand constraints and clobber lists
   - Generate appropriate LLVM inline assembly instructions

4. Foreign Function Interface (FFI):
   - Implement C ABI compatibility layer
   - Add support for `extern "C"` function declarations
   - Create mechanisms for marshaling data between Goo and C types
   - Support dynamic library loading and symbol resolution

5. Hardware Control Primitives:
   - Implement memory-mapped I/O operations
   - Add port I/O instructions (in/out on x86)
   - Support interrupt handling mechanisms
   - Implement atomic operations for hardware synchronization

6. Kernel-Specific Memory Management:
   - Add page table manipulation primitives
   - Implement physical memory allocation interfaces
   - Support for memory protection and permission settings
   - Create safe abstractions around unsafe memory operations

7. LLVM IR Generation:
   - Extend the LLVM IR code generator to handle unsafe operations
   - Generate appropriate LLVM intrinsics for hardware operations
   - Implement inline assembly translation to LLVM IR
   - Ensure proper optimization barriers around volatile operations

8. Safety Mechanisms:
   - Implement compile-time warnings for unsafe code usage
   - Add runtime checks at unsafe/safe boundaries where possible
   - Create linting tools to identify potentially dangerous patterns
   - Document best practices for using unsafe features safely

Code examples:

```go
// Raw pointer operations
unsafe {
    ptr := (*int)(unsafe.Pointer(uintptr(0x1000)))
    *ptr = 42
    ptr = ptr + 1  // pointer arithmetic
}

// Inline assembly
unsafe {
    asm {
        "mov $42, %eax"
        "ret"
        : "=a"(result)
        :
        : "memory"
    }
}

// Memory-mapped I/O
unsafe {
    mmioReg := (*volatile uint32)(unsafe.Pointer(uintptr(0xFFFF0000)))
    *mmioReg = 0x1  // Write to hardware register
    value := *mmioReg  // Read from hardware register
}

// Port I/O (x86)
unsafe {
    outb(0x64, 0xFE)  // Send reset command to keyboard controller
}
```

tasks.title: Implement GPU Programming Support
tasks.description: Add GPU backend integration with CUDA/OpenCL, implement compute shader compilation targets, GPU memory management, kernel execution, and support for multiple GPU architectures (NVPTX, AMDGPU, SPIR-V, and Metal).
tasks.details:
This task involves extending the compiler to support GPU programming through multiple backends:

1. LLVM Integration for GPU Targets:
   - Extend the LLVM IR code generator to support GPU-specific intrinsics and attributes
   - Implement target-specific code generation for NVPTX, AMDGPU, SPIR-V, and Metal Shading Language
   - Add compilation pipelines for each GPU architecture

2. GPU Memory Management:
   - Implement ownership semantics for GPU memory allocations
   - Create abstractions for device memory, shared memory, and constant memory
   - Add runtime support for memory transfers between host and device
   - Implement memory pool and caching strategies for efficient reuse

3. Kernel Programming Model:
   - Design language extensions for kernel functions with GPU-specific attributes
   - Support thread/block/grid abstractions for CUDA-like programming
   - Implement work-group and work-item abstractions for OpenCL-like programming
   - Add annotations for compute shader entry points

4. Runtime Library:
   - Develop a runtime API for device initialization, context management
   - Implement kernel launch mechanisms with parameter marshaling
   - Create synchronization primitives for GPU execution
   - Add profiling and error handling capabilities

5. Optimization Passes:
   - Implement GPU-specific optimization passes (memory coalescing, shared memory usage)
   - Add vectorization support for GPU SIMD units
   - Create loop transformations specific to GPU execution models
   - Implement automatic kernel fusion where possible

6. Code Examples:
```go
// Example kernel function in Goo
@kernel
func vectorAdd(a, b, c []float32, n int) {
    // Get the global thread ID
    i := blockIdx.x * blockDim.x + threadIdx.x
    if i < n {
        c[i] = a[i] + b[i]
    }
}

// Example kernel launch
func main() {
    // Allocate device memory
    d_a := cuda.Malloc[float32](N)
    d_b := cuda.Malloc[float32](N)
    d_c := cuda.Malloc[float32](N)
    
    // Copy data to device
    cuda.Memcpy(d_a, a, N, cuda.HostToDevice)
    cuda.Memcpy(d_b, b, N, cuda.HostToDevice)
    
    // Launch kernel
    blockSize := 256
    gridSize := (N + blockSize - 1) / blockSize
    vectorAdd<<<gridSize, blockSize>>>(d_a, d_b, d_c, N)
    
    // Copy result back
    cuda.Memcpy(c, d_c, N, cuda.DeviceToHost)
    
    // Free device memory
    cuda.Free(d_a)
    cuda.Free(d_b)
    cuda.Free(d_c)
}
```

Testing GPU programming support requires a comprehensive approach across multiple levels:

1. Unit Tests:
   - Create unit tests for each GPU backend component (memory management, kernel launch, etc.)

tasks.title: Implement WebAssembly Support
tasks.description: Add WebAssembly as a compilation target through the LLVM backend, enabling Goo programs to run in browsers and other WASM environments with full interoperability.
tasks.details:
Implement WebAssembly support for the Goo compiler by leveraging the existing LLVM IR code generator and adding WASM-specific functionality:

1. LLVM WASM Target Integration:
   - Configure LLVM backend to target WebAssembly (wasm32)
   - Add WASM-specific optimization passes
   - Implement WASM binary emission and validation

2. Linear Memory Model:
   - Implement WebAssembly's linear memory model
   - Add bounds checking for memory access
   - Create memory management utilities for WASM heap
   - Handle memory growth operations

3. Concurrency Model:
   - Implement cooperative concurrency for single-threaded environments
   - Create event loop integration for asynchronous operations
   - Adapt Goo's concurrency primitives (channels, goroutines) to WASM's execution model
   - Implement task scheduling compatible with browser event loops

4. JavaScript Interoperability:
   - Create bidirectional type marshaling between Goo and JavaScript
   - Implement function call bridging
   - Support for JavaScript callbacks from Goo code
   - Handle primitive types, objects, arrays, and functions
   - Add special handling for DOM objects and browser APIs

5. Runtime Environment Support:
   - Implement browser environment detection and adaptation
   - Add Node.js runtime support
   - Support WASI (WebAssembly System Interface) for non-browser environments
   - Create environment-specific APIs and polyfills

6. DOM Integration:
   - Create type definitions for DOM APIs
   - Implement DOM event handling
   - Add support for DOM manipulation from Goo code
   - Handle browser-specific features and compatibility

7. Build System Integration:
   - Update compiler flags and options for WASM target
   - Create WASM-specific linking and bundling tools
   - Add configuration for different WASM environments
   - Implement source maps for debugging

Example WASM target configuration:
```go
func initWasmTarget(ctx context.Context, llvmModule *llvm.Module) error {
    triple := "wasm32-unknown-unknown"
    llvmModule.SetTarget(triple)
    
    // Configure data layout for WebAssembly
    dataLayout := "e-m:e-p:32:32-i64:64-n32:64-S128"
    llvmModule.SetDataLayout(dataLayout)
    
    // Add WASM-specific attributes
    return nil
}
```

Example JavaScript interop function:
```go
// Export a Goo function to JavaScript
func exportFunction(name string, fn *ast.FuncDecl, module *llvm.Module) error {
    // Mark function as exported in WASM
    llvmFunc := module.GetNamedFunction(name)
    if llvmFunc.IsNil() {
        return fmt.Errorf("function %s not found", name)
    }
    
    llvmFunc.SetLinkage(llvm.ExternalLinkage)
    llvmFunc.AddFunctionAttr(llvm.CreateStringAttribute("wasm-export-name", name))
    
    return nil
}
```

tasks.title: Implement Standard Library Foundation
tasks.description: Create core modules (io, fmt, strings), collections (vec, map, set), network programming with channel patterns, OS interfaces, and essential utilities that provide a solid foundation for Goo applications.
tasks.testStrategy:
To verify the correct implementation of the Standard Library Foundation, follow these testing steps:

1. Unit Testing:
   - Create comprehensive unit tests for each module and function
   - Test both normal operation and error conditions
   - Verify that ownership semantics are correctly implemented
   - Example for testing the `strings` package:
     ```go
     func TestStringSplit() {
         result := strings.Split("a,b,c", ",")
         assert.Equal([]string{"a", "b", "c"}, result)
         
         result = strings.Split("abc", ",")
         assert.Equal([]string{"abc"}, result)
         
         result = strings.Split("", ",")
         assert.Equal([]string{""}, result)
     }
     ```

2. Integration Testing:
   - Create test programs that use multiple standard library components together
   - Verify that components interact correctly
   - Test for memory leaks and ownership violations
   - Example integration test:
     ```go
     func TestFileReadAndParse() {
         // Create a temporary file
         file, err := os.Create("test.txt")
         assert.NoError(err)
         
         // Write to the file
         _, err = file.Write([]byte("line1\nline2\nline3"))
         assert.NoError(err)
         file.Close()
         
         // Read the file
         content, err := os.ReadFile("test.txt")
         assert.NoError(err)
         
         // Parse the content
         lines := strings.Split(string(content), "\n")
         assert.Equal([]string{"line1", "line2", "line3"}, lines)
         
         // Clean up
         os.Remove("test.txt")
     }
     ```

3. Performance Testing:
   - Benchmark critical operations in each module
   - Compare performance against similar operations in Go
   - Ensure collections perform efficiently for various sizes
   - Example benchmark:
     ```go
     func BenchmarkVecPush() {
         v := vec.New[int]()
         for i := 0; i < 1000000; i++ {
             v.Push(i)
         }
     }
     ```

4. Memory Safety Testing:
   - Verify that memory is properly managed according to Goo's ownership rules
   - Test for memory leaks using tools like Valgrind
   - Ensure that borrowed references don't outlive their owners
   - Example memory safety test:
     ```go
     func TestMapOwnership() {
         m := map.New[string, vec.Vec[int]]()
         
         // Create and insert a vector
         v := vec.New[int]()
         v.Push(1)
         v.Push(2)
         
         // Transfer ownership to the map
         m.Insert("key", v)
         
         // v should no longer be usable if ownership was transferred
         // This should cause a compile-time error in Goo
         // v.Push(3) // Should not compile
         
         // Get a borrowed reference
         borrowed := m.GetRef("key")
         assert.Equal(2, borrowed.Len())
         
         // Borrowed reference should not allow mutation
         // borrowed.Push(3) // Should not compile
     }
     ```

5. Error Handling Testing:
   - Verify that error unions are correctly implemented
   - Test error propagation across module boundaries
   - Ensure that nullable types are handled properly
   - Example error handling test:
     ```go
     func TestFileOpenError() {
         // Try to open a non-existent file
         _, err := os.Open("nonexistent.txt")
         
         // Check that we got an error
         assert.Error(err)
         
         // Test error handling with match statement
         match err {
             case os.ErrNotExist:
                 // Expected case
                 assert.True(true)
             default:
                 assert.Fail("Expected ErrNotExist")
         }
     }
     ```

6. Cross-Platform Testing:
   - Test the standard library on multiple platforms (Linux, macOS, Windows)
   - Verify that OS-specific functionality works correctly
   - Ensure consistent behavior across platforms where appropriate
   - Document platform-specific differences

7. Documentation Testing:
   - Verify that all examples in the documentation compile and run correctly
   - Test that the documentation is complete for all public APIs
   - Ensure that ownership and error handling patterns are clearly documented

8. End-to-End Testing:
   - Create sample applications that use the standard library extensively
   - Verify that these applications work correctly
   - Example applications could include:
     - A simple HTTP server
     - A file processing utility
     - A network client/server application using channel patterns

tasks.title: Implement LughOS Integration Framework
tasks.description: Create a freestanding runtime with no_std support, hardware abstraction layer (HAL), boot sequence, and kernel services architecture to enable Goo language integration with embedded and OS development.
tasks.details:
This task involves implementing the core components needed for LughOS integration, allowing Goo to be used for operating system and embedded systems development:

1. Freestanding Runtime with no_std Support:
   - Implement a minimal runtime that can operate without the standard library
   - Create core memory primitives that work without an underlying OS
   - Develop bare-metal allocation strategies (bump allocator, free list allocator)
   - Implement core data structures needed for kernel development

2. Hardware Abstraction Layer (HAL):
   - Define trait interfaces for hardware components (CPU, MMU, timers, interrupts)
   - Implement architecture-specific HAL modules (starting with x86_64)
   - Create memory-mapped I/O abstractions
   - Develop device driver interfaces and registration system

3. Boot Sequence and Initialization:
   - Implement multiboot2-compatible entry point
   - Create early initialization routines (stack setup, BSS clearing)
   - Develop CPU feature detection and initialization
   - Implement platform detection and hardware enumeration

4. Interrupt Descriptor Table (IDT) Management:
   - Create IDT data structures and setup routines
   - Implement interrupt handler registration system
   - Develop exception handlers for CPU faults
   - Add support for custom interrupt handlers

5. Memory Management Unit (MMU) Control:
   - Implement page table manipulation functions
   - Create virtual memory mapping abstractions
   - Develop memory protection mechanisms
   - Implement TLB management functions

6. Kernel Services Architecture:
   - Design modular kernel service interfaces
   - Implement inter-service communication mechanisms
   - Create service registration and discovery system
   - Develop service lifecycle management

7. Capability-Based Security System:
   - Implement capability data structures and validation
   - Create capability delegation and revocation mechanisms
   - Develop object capability references
   - Implement capability-based access control

8. Example Microkernel Implementation:
   - Create a minimal working microkernel using the framework
   - Implement process isolation and scheduling
   - Add IPC mechanisms (message passing, shared memory)
   - Develop a simple device driver model

The implementation should leverage Goo's unsafe blocks and hardware control primitives to interact directly with hardware while maintaining safety guarantees in non-unsafe code. The framework should be designed to be modular, allowing developers to use only the components they need for their specific OS or embedded project.

tasks.title: Implement Zero-Effort Memory Safety
tasks.description: Create automatic memory management without borrow checker complexity, providing Rust-level safety with Go-level simplicity.
tasks.details:
Implement an advanced memory safety system that eliminates the need for explicit borrow checking while maintaining memory safety guarantees:

1. Automatic Ownership Inference:
   - Implement flow-sensitive ownership analysis
   - Automatically infer move vs copy semantics based on usage patterns
   - Eliminate need for explicit lifetime annotations
   - Generate optimal move/copy operations without programmer intervention
   - Leverage the fixed memory management in type_function as a foundation

2. Smart Reference Management:
   ```goo
   // No lifetime annotations needed - compiler handles everything
   func create_processor() DataProcessor {
       config := Config{...}
       return DataProcessor{config} // Automatically handles ownership
   }
   
   // Automatic memory safety without mental overhead
   func process_data() {
       data := [1, 2, 3]
       slice := data[:]       // Safe reference
       data = append(data, 4) // Just works - compiler ensures safety
       process(slice)         // Automatically safe
   }
   ```

3. Advanced Escape Analysis:
   - Implement interprocedural escape analysis
   - Automatically determine stack vs heap allocation
   - Optimize memory layout based on usage patterns
   - Eliminate unnecessary heap allocations

4. Automatic Resource Management:
   - RAII-style resource cleanup without explicit destructors
   - Automatic cleanup of file handles, network connections, etc.
   - Exception-safe resource management
   - Deterministic cleanup ordering

5. Memory Layout Optimization:
   - Automatic struct packing and alignment
   - Cache-friendly data structure layout
   - Automatic memory pooling for frequent allocations
   - NUMA-aware memory allocation

6. Integration with Type System:
   - Extend type system to track memory safety properties
   - Compile-time verification of memory safety
   - Integration with error unions and nullable types
   - Automatic null pointer dereference prevention
tasks.testStrategy:
1. Memory Safety Verification:
   - Create comprehensive test suite for memory safety violations
   - Test with complex data structures and ownership patterns
   - Verify prevention of use-after-free and double-free errors

subtasks.title: Implement Flow-Sensitive Ownership Analysis
subtasks.description: Develop the core ownership inference system that automatically determines when values should be moved or copied based on their usage patterns in the code.
subtasks.details: Create a flow-sensitive analysis pass that tracks variable usage throughout function bodies. Implement algorithms to detect when variables are used after potential moves, when they're captured by closures, and when they're passed to functions. Build a constraint solver that determines the minimal set of copy operations needed for correctness. Integrate with the existing type system to propagate ownership information across function boundaries.
subtasks.testStrategy: Create unit tests with various ownership patterns including simple moves, conditional moves, moves in loops, and complex nested structures. Verify that the compiler correctly identifies when copies are needed and when moves are safe.

subtasks.title: Implement Smart Reference Management
subtasks.description: Create a system that automatically manages references to ensure they remain valid throughout their lifetime without requiring explicit lifetime annotations.
subtasks.details: Extend the ownership analysis to track references and their targets. Implement borrow checking logic that verifies references don't outlive their targets. Add compiler-generated code to maintain reference validity during mutations. Create specialized handling for slices and other view types to ensure they remain valid when their underlying data is modified. Implement automatic reference counting or region-based memory management as needed.
subtasks.testStrategy: Test with scenarios involving references to stack and heap data, references to struct fields, slices that might be invalidated by append operations, and references captured in closures.

subtasks.title: Implement Interprocedural Escape Analysis
subtasks.description: Build an advanced escape analysis system that determines optimal memory allocation strategies across function boundaries.
subtasks.details: Create an interprocedural analysis pass that tracks object lifetimes across function calls. Implement algorithms to determine when objects can be stack-allocated versus heap-allocated. Build heuristics for optimizing memory layout based on access patterns. Create specialized handling for closures and function arguments. Implement region-based allocation for objects with similar lifetimes.
subtasks.testStrategy: Create benchmarks comparing performance against manual memory management. Test with complex data structures, nested function calls, and scenarios with varying object lifetimes. Verify that objects are allocated in the optimal location.

subtasks.title: Implement Automatic Resource Management
subtasks.description: Create a system for automatically managing non-memory resources like file handles, network connections, and locks with deterministic cleanup.
subtasks.details: Implement RAII-style resource management that automatically inserts cleanup code at the end of scopes. Create a resource tracking system that ensures deterministic cleanup even in the presence of early returns and errors. Implement special handling for resources in loops and conditionals. Add compiler support for defer-like functionality that's tied to variable scopes rather than function returns. Create interfaces for custom resource types to hook into the automatic management system.
subtasks.testStrategy: Test with various resource types including files, network connections, and locks. Verify resources are properly cleaned up in complex control flows including early returns, panics, and nested scopes.

subtasks.title: Integrate Memory Safety with Type System
subtasks.description: Extend the type system to track and enforce memory safety properties at compile time, preventing null dereferences and use-after-free errors.
subtasks.details: Enhance the type system to track nullability, ownership, and borrowing information. Implement compile-time checks that prevent common memory safety errors. Create specialized handling for error unions and optional types. Implement automatic null checking before dereferences. Add support for compile-time verification of resource cleanup. Create clear error messages that guide developers toward memory-safe code without requiring deep understanding of the underlying system.
subtasks.testStrategy: Create a comprehensive test suite that attempts various memory-unsafe operations and verifies they're caught at compile time. Test integration with the error handling system and nullable types. Verify that safe code patterns compile without unnecessary restrictions.

This should include: 1) Modifying the error union type to store contextual information, 2) Enhancing the 'try' keyword to automatically capture source location, function name, and relevant variable values, 3) Creating a data structure to maintain the error chain, and 4) Ensuring memory management is handled correctly for the error context chain.

       if @target_has("avx2") {

       } else if @target_has("aes_ni") {

       } else {

   ```

3. Intelligent Version Resolution:

   ```

5. Performance-Guided Selection:

6. Dependency Analysis:

subtasks.details: Develop a resolver that parses import statements like `import "json"` and selects the optimal library based on predefined criteria. Implement a metadata system for packages that includes performance metrics, compatibility information, and feature sets. Create a scoring algorithm that ranks available packages based on target platform, performance needs, and compatibility with the project. Ensure the resolver can handle generic imports (e.g., 'json') and more specific imports (e.g., 'http/client').

Implement the `@target_has()` function that evaluates to a boolean at compile time. Develop a mechanism for conditional imports within comptime blocks. Create a compatibility analysis system that can predict whether packages will work together based on their interfaces and historical compatibility data. tasks.title: Implement Automatic Parallelization System
tasks.description: Create a compiler system that automatically parallelizes code when beneficial, including SIMD vectorization and multi-core distribution with performance guarantees.
tasks.details:
Implement an automatic parallelization system that analyzes code and applies parallelization techniques when performance benefits are expected:

1. Annotation System:
   ```goo
   @auto_parallel
   func processData(data []float64) []float64 {
       result := make([]float64, len(data))
       for i := 0; i < len(data); i++ {
           result[i] = complexComputation(data[i])
       }
       return result
   }
   ```

2. Data Dependency Analysis:
   - Implement static analysis to detect data dependencies between loop iterations
   - Build dependency graphs to identify parallelizable regions
   - Analyze array access patterns to detect opportunities for vectorization
   - Implement alias analysis to determine when memory regions may overlap

3. Loop Parallelization:
   - Implement loop splitting for partially parallelizable loops
   - Support loop tiling for cache optimization
   - Implement loop unrolling for vectorization preparation
   - Add automatic reduction detection (sum, product, min/max)

4. Function-Level Parallelization:
   - Detect independent function calls that can run concurrently
   - Implement task-based parallelism with work-stealing scheduler
   - Add automatic task granularity control

5. SIMD Vectorization:
   - Generate SIMD instructions for supported operations
   - Support multiple instruction sets (AVX-512, NEON, SSE, etc.)
   - Implement automatic fallback for unsupported hardware
   - Add vector lane predication for handling edge cases

6. Hardware-Specific Optimization:
   - Detect available hardware capabilities at compile time
   - Generate specialized code paths for different hardware
   - Optimize memory access patterns for specific architectures
   - Add cache-aware optimizations

7. Performance Guarantees:
   - Implement cost models to predict parallelization benefits
   - Add runtime fallback when predictions are uncertain
   - Provide compile-time warnings for potentially inefficient parallelization
   - Support user-defined thresholds for automatic parallelization

8. Integration with Existing Systems:
   - Connect with the LLVM IR generator for low-level optimization
   - Leverage compile-time execution for static analysis
   - Use type information to guide parallelization decisions

The system should be conservative by default, only parallelizing when clear performance benefits are expected, with options for more aggressive parallelization when requested by the developer.

Note: Leverage the fixed memory management in type_function to ensure proper memory handling in parallelized code.
tasks.testStrategy:
1. Unit Testing:
   - Create unit tests for each component of the parallelization system
   - Test dependency analysis with known patterns (independent, reduction, etc.)
   - Verify correct SIMD code generation for different instruction sets
   - Test the annotation parser and semantic analyzer
   - Verify memory management in parallelized code

2. Benchmark Suite:
   - Develop a comprehensive benchmark suite with various parallelization patterns
   - Include compute-bound and memory-bound workloads
   - Test with different data sizes to evaluate scaling behavior
   - Compare performance against manually optimized code

3. Correctness Verification:
   - Run parallelized code against sequential versions to verify identical results
   - Test edge cases (empty arrays, single elements, large datasets)
   - Verify correct handling of reductions and other special patterns
   - Test with deliberately challenging cases (e.g., complex dependencies)

4. Performance Testing:
   - Measure speedup across different core counts
   - Test on various hardware configurations
   - Verify that the cost model correctly predicts when parallelization is beneficial
   - Ensure no performance regressions on single-threaded code

5. Integration Testing:
   - Verify correct interaction with the LLVM IR generator
   - Test integration with the compile-time execution system
   - Ensure proper error reporting for non-parallelizable code
   - Test with real-world applications and libraries

6. Hardware-Specific Testing:
   - Test on different CPU architectures (x86, ARM, etc.)
   - Verify correct detection and utilization of SIMD capabilities
   - Test fallback mechanisms when hardware features are unavailable

7. Regression Testing:
   - Create a test suite that can be run automatically for each change
   - Include previously discovered edge cases and bugs
   - Measure performance impact of changes over time

tasks.title: Implement Interactive Development Environment
tasks.description: Create a comprehensive IDE experience for Goo with hot reload capabilities, REPL with type information, real-time performance hints, automatic error fixes, and interactive debugging with time-travel capabilities.
tasks.details:
Implement a modern interactive development environment for Goo that enhances developer productivity through real-time feedback and interactive features:

1. Hot Reload System:
   - Implement `@hot_reload` annotation for marking functions and types that support live code updates
   - Create a background compiler service that watches for file changes and recompiles incrementally
   - Develop state preservation mechanism to maintain program state during reloads
   ```goo
   @hot_reload
   func renderUI(data: AppState) {
       // This function can be modified while the program is running
       // Changes will be applied without restarting the application
   }
   ```
   - Implement serialization/deserialization of program state for persistence between reloads
   - Add hooks for custom state migration when type definitions change

2. REPL with Full Type Information:
   - Create an interactive REPL that provides immediate feedback with rich type information
   - Implement syntax highlighting and code completion in the REPL
   - Display inferred types for expressions and variables
   - Support importing project modules and accessing project state
   ```
   goo> x := fetchUserData()
   // Result: User{id: 42, name: "Alice", roles: ["admin", "developer"]}
   // Type: User (from module "app/models")
   ```
   - Enable exploration of object properties and method signatures
   - Provide documentation lookup directly from the REPL

3. Real-time Performance Hints:
   - Implement runtime performance monitoring that provides feedback in the IDE
   - Highlight code sections with potential performance issues (memory allocations, lock contention)
   - Show estimated computational complexity for functions
   - Provide suggestions for performance improvements based on actual runtime data
   - Display memory usage patterns and garbage collection impact
   - Integrate with the compiler to show optimization opportunities

4. Automatic Error Fixes:
   - Implement an AI-assisted error correction system that suggests fixes for common errors
   - Create quick-fix actions for type errors, null safety violations, and ownership issues
   - Provide refactoring suggestions for improving code quality
   - Implement automated migration tools for API changes
   - Support batch fixing of similar issues across the codebase

5. Interactive Debugging:
   - Implement time-travel debugging that allows stepping backward through execution history
   - Create visualization tools for complex data structures
   - Support conditional breakpoints with rich expressions
   - Implement value tracking across execution timeline
   - Add ability to modify variables during debugging and see effects propagate
   - Create snapshot system for saving and comparing program states
   ```goo
   // Debug configuration
   debug.timeTravel {
       maxSteps: 1000,
       watchExpressions: ["user.balance", "transaction.status"],
       captureFrequency: 100ms,
   }
   ```

6. IDE Integration:
   - Develop Language Server Protocol (LSP) extensions for Goo-specific features
   - Create plugins for major IDEs (VSCode, JetBrains, Vim/Emacs)
   - Implement custom UI components for visualizing Goo-specific concepts
   - Support remote development and debugging
   - Integrate with the package manager and build system from Task 17

7. Development Workflow Enhancements:
   - Create project templates and wizards for common application types
   - Implement integrated testing tools with visual test results
   - Add profiling visualization directly in the editor
   - Support pair programming and collaborative editing
   - Implement integrated documentation generation and preview

Note: Leverage the completed automatic constraint inference system to provide accurate type information and suggestions in the IDE.

   - Validate that runtime monitoring has minimal impact on application performance

tasks.title: Security Analysis Framework Implementation
tasks.description: Implement comprehensive security vulnerability detection and side-channel attack analysis capabilities for the Goo compiler to identify potential security weaknesses in code.
tasks.details:
Implement a comprehensive security analysis framework that builds upon the existing Security by Design Framework to detect vulnerabilities and potential side-channel attacks in Goo code:

1. **Vulnerability Detection System**:
   - Implement static analysis patterns for common vulnerabilities (buffer overflows, injection attacks, etc.)
   - Create a plugin architecture for custom vulnerability detectors
   - Integrate with the compiler's AST to analyze code flow and data handling
   - Add severity classification for detected issues
   ```goo
   // Example vulnerability pattern definition
   vulnerability BufferOverflow {
     pattern: "array_access(arr, idx) where !bounds_checked(idx, arr.length)",
     severity: Critical,
     remediation: "Use safe indexing or add explicit bounds check"
   }
   ```

2. **Side-Channel Attack Analysis**:
   - Implement timing attack detection by analyzing code paths with conditional branches on sensitive data
   - Add power analysis vulnerability detection for cryptographic operations
   - Create cache attack detection for security-critical functions
   - Implement constant-time operation verification for cryptographic primitives
   ```goo
   // Example annotation for constant-time requirements
   @constant_time
   func compare_hmac(a: []byte, b: []byte) bool {
     // Implementation will be verified for timing consistency
   }
   ```

3. **Integration with Compile-Time Execution**:
   - Leverage compile-time execution to perform security proofs on critical code
   - Implement symbolic execution for vulnerability detection during compilation
   - Create security property verification using compile-time assertions
   ```goo
   comptime {
     verify_no_timing_leaks(encrypt_function)
     prove_memory_safety(buffer_handling_function)
   }
   ```

4. **Security Reporting System**:
   - Generate detailed security reports with vulnerability descriptions and remediation steps
   - Implement security metrics calculation (CVSS scores, attack surface estimation)
   - Create visualization of data flow highlighting potential security issues
   - Add integration with common security tracking systems (JIRA, GitHub Security)

5. **Taint Analysis Extensions**:
   - Extend the existing taint analysis system to track data flow across module boundaries
   - Implement context-sensitive taint analysis for higher precision
   - Add support for custom taint propagation rules
   - Create sanitizer functions that can remove taint from properly validated data
   ```goo
   // Example sanitizer function
   @sanitizer
   func validate_input(input: @tainted string) !string {
     // Validation logic that returns untainted string or error
   }
   ```

6. **Runtime Security Checks**:
   - Generate runtime assertions for security properties that cannot be verified statically
   - Implement security-focused fuzzing capabilities that can be enabled during testing
   - Create canary values and runtime integrity checks for critical data structures
   - Add optional runtime taint tracking for dynamic security analysis
tasks.testStrategy:
The security analysis framework should be thoroughly tested using the following approach:

1. **Vulnerability Detection Testing**:
   - Create a comprehensive test suite with examples of common vulnerabilities
   - Verify that the system correctly identifies all implemented vulnerability patterns
   - Test false positive rates on secure code samples
   - Benchmark performance impact of vulnerability scanning on compilation time
   - Verify that remediation suggestions are accurate and implementable

2. **Side-Channel Analysis Testing**:
   - Create test cases with known timing vulnerabilities
   - Verify detection of non-constant time operations in cryptographic code
   - Test with different optimization levels to ensure consistent detection
   - Create benchmark suite for measuring actual timing differences in identified code
   - Validate that properly implemented constant-time operations pass verification

3. **Integration Testing**:
   - Test integration with the existing Security by Design Framework
   - Verify that compile-time security proofs correctly identify issues
   - Test symbolic execution on complex code examples
   - Verify that security annotations are properly respected during compilation
   - Test interaction with other compiler features like null safety and ownership

4. **Reporting System Testing**:
   - Verify accuracy and completeness of generated security reports
   - Test CVSS score calculation against reference implementations
   - Validate visualization output for different vulnerability types
   - Test integration with external security tracking systems
   - Verify that reports include actionable remediation steps

5. **Taint Analysis Testing**:
   - Create complex data flow scenarios to test taint propagation
   - Verify context-sensitive analysis correctly handles different execution paths
   - Test custom taint propagation rules for accuracy
   - Verify sanitizer functions properly remove taint when used correctly
   - Test taint analysis across module boundaries and with third-party code

6. **Runtime Security Testing**:
   - Verify that generated runtime assertions correctly catch security violations
   - Test performance impact of runtime security checks
   - Validate fuzzing capabilities find actual vulnerabilities
   - Test canary value effectiveness against buffer overflows
   - Verify runtime taint tracking correctly identifies unsafe data flows

7. **Real-World Testing**:
   - Apply the security analysis framework to existing Goo codebases
   - Compare results with manual security audits to validate effectiveness
   - Test against known vulnerable code patterns from other languages
   - Verify detection of real-world vulnerabilities from CVE database

tasks.title: Implement Goo Runtime Library
tasks.description: Build a minimal runtime library for Goo that implements all runtime functions referenced in LLVM IR, including initialization, memory management, I/O operations, error handling, and string/slice operations.
tasks.details:
Implement a comprehensive static library in C that provides all runtime functions needed by compiled Goo programs:

1. Program Lifecycle Management:
   - `void goo_init(int argc, char** argv)`: Initialize the runtime environment, process command-line arguments
   - `void goo_exit(int code)`: Clean up resources and terminate program with exit code

2. Memory Management:
   - `void* goo_alloc(size_t size)`: Allocate memory with proper alignment and error handling
   - `void goo_free(void* ptr)`: Free allocated memory with safety checks
   - `void* goo_realloc(void* ptr, size_t new_size)`: Resize existing allocations

3. I/O Operations:
   - `void goo_print(const char* str)`: Print string to standard output
   - `void goo_println(const char* str)`: Print string with newline to standard output
   - `void goo_eprint(const char* str)`: Print string to standard error
   - `void goo_eprintln(const char* str)`: Print string with newline to standard error
   - `char* goo_read_line()`: Read a line from standard input

4. Error Handling:
   - `void goo_panic(const char* message)`: Terminate program with error message
   - `void goo_error_create(const char* message, int code)`: Create error object
   - `void goo_error_propagate(void* error)`: Propagate error up the call stack

5. String Operations:
   - `char* goo_string_concat(const char* str1, const char* str2)`: Concatenate strings
   - `int goo_string_compare(const char* str1, const char* str2)`: Compare strings
   - `char* goo_string_substring(const char* str, int start, int length)`: Extract substring

6. Slice Operations:
   - `void* goo_slice_create(size_t element_size, size_t capacity)`: Create a new slice
   - `void goo_slice_append(void* slice, void* element)`: Append element to slice
   - `void* goo_slice_get(void* slice, size_t index)`: Get element at index
   - `void goo_slice_set(void* slice, size_t index, void* element)`: Set element at index

7. Runtime Type Information:
   - `int goo_type_check(void* value, int type_id)`: Check if value matches type
   - `int goo_type_id(void* value)`: Get runtime type ID of value

8. Build System Integration:
   - Create a static library (.a file) that can be linked with compiled Goo programs
   - Provide header files with all function declarations
   - Ensure compatibility with the LLVM IR generated by the compiler

The implementation should be optimized for performance while maintaining safety, with careful attention to memory management to prevent leaks and corruption.
tasks.testStrategy:
1. Unit Testing:
   - Create comprehensive unit tests for each runtime function
   - Test each function with valid inputs, edge cases, and error conditions
   - Verify memory management functions correctly handle allocation, reallocation, and deallocation
   - Test string and slice operations with various inputs including empty strings, large strings, and special characters

2. Integration Testing:
   - Create simple Goo programs that use each runtime function
   - Compile these programs to LLVM IR and link with the runtime library
   - Verify correct execution and expected output
   - Test with programs that stress memory management, I/O, and error handling

3. Memory Safety Testing:
   - Use tools like Valgrind and AddressSanitizer to detect memory leaks and errors
   - Create test cases that exercise boundary conditions in memory management
   - Verify no memory leaks occur during normal operation and error conditions

4. Performance Testing:
   - Benchmark key operations like memory allocation, string manipulation, and I/O
   - Compare performance against similar runtime libraries
   - Optimize critical paths based on benchmark results

5. Cross-Platform Testing:
   - Verify the runtime library works correctly on all target platforms (Linux, macOS, Windows)
   - Test with different compilers (GCC, Clang) and optimization levels
   - Ensure consistent behavior across platforms

6. Error Handling Testing:
   - Verify error propagation works correctly across function boundaries
   - Test recovery from error conditions
   - Ensure error messages are clear and helpful

7. Documentation Verification:
   - Ensure all functions are properly documented with parameters, return values, and error conditions
   - Verify header files contain accurate function declarations
   - Create example usage documentation for each function

tasks.title: Fix Type Size Mismatches in Code Generation
tasks.description: Identify and correct type size mismatches in code generation where i64 values are being stored into i32 variables, ensuring proper alignment between Goo types and LLVM types.
tasks.testStrategy:
To verify the correct implementation of type size mismatch fixes:

1. Static Analysis Testing:
   - Create a static analyzer that checks for potential type size mismatches
   - Run the analyzer on the entire codebase to identify all instances
   - Verify all identified issues have been properly addressed

2. Unit Testing:
   - Create unit tests for each type of conversion (i64 to i32, etc.)
   - Test edge cases where values are at the boundaries of type ranges
   - Verify truncation warnings are emitted when appropriate
   - Test platform-specific type handling on different architectures

3. Integration Testing:
   - Compile test programs that exercise various type conversions
   - Verify generated LLVM IR contains appropriate type casts
   - Test programs that use large integer values to ensure proper handling
   - Verify struct field alignment is correct across different platforms

4. Regression Testing:
   - Create a test suite that captures all previously identified type mismatch bugs
   - Ensure all regression tests pass after the fixes
   - Verify no new type-related issues are introduced

5. Cross-Platform Verification:
   - Test on both 32-bit and 64-bit platforms
   - Verify correct behavior on different operating systems
   - Test with different LLVM versions to ensure compatibility

6. Performance Testing:
   - Benchmark before and after to ensure type conversions don't impact performance
   - Verify that explicit casts don't introduce unnecessary overhead
   - Test with optimizations enabled to ensure conversions are properly optimized

7. Example Test Cases:
   ```go
   func testTypeSizeMismatches() {
     // Test i64 to i32 conversion
     var large: i64 = 0x100000000
     var small: i32 = large as i32  // Should generate warning
     assert(small == 0)  // Truncation occurred
     
     // Test pointer to integer conversion
     var ptr: *void = nil
     var addr: uintptr = ptr as uintptr  // Should maintain full pointer width
     
     // Test platform-specific integer sizes
     var platform_int: int = 0xFFFFFFFF
     if sizeof(int) == 8 {
       assert(platform_int == 0xFFFFFFFF)
     } else {
       assert(platform_int == -1)  // Overflow on 32-bit platforms
     }
   }
   ```

tasks.title: Fix Parser Warnings and Clean Up Yacc Grammar
tasks.description: Resolve parser warnings about useless nonterminals and reduce/reduce conflicts by cleaning up the Yacc grammar file to create a more robust and maintainable parsing system.
tasks.details:
This task involves improving the parser's grammar definition to eliminate warnings and conflicts:

1. Analyze the current Yacc grammar file to identify:
   - Useless nonterminals (grammar rules that are defined but never used)
   - Reduce/reduce conflicts (ambiguities where the parser can't decide which rule to apply)
   - Shift/reduce conflicts that can be resolved

2. Clean up the grammar by:
   - Removing unused grammar rules and nonterminals
   - Restructuring ambiguous grammar rules to eliminate reduce/reduce conflicts
   - Adding precedence directives where appropriate to resolve shift/reduce conflicts
   - Ensuring all grammar rules are reachable from the start symbol
   - Documenting complex grammar constructs with clear comments

3. Refactor the grammar to improve maintainability:
   - Group related grammar rules together
   - Add clear section headers for different language constructs
   - Ensure consistent naming conventions for nonterminals
   - Simplify overly complex grammar rules by breaking them into smaller, more focused rules

4. Update any associated parser code that might be affected by grammar changes:
   - Ensure semantic actions are properly updated
   - Verify that AST generation remains consistent
   - Update any error handling code that depends on specific grammar rules

5. Document all changes made to the grammar, especially:
   - Rules that were removed and why
   - Rules that were restructured to resolve conflicts
   - New precedence directives added
tasks.testStrategy:
1. Run the parser with verbose output to verify that all warnings about useless nonterminals have been eliminated

2. Use parser debugging tools to confirm that reduce/reduce conflicts have been resolved:
   - Run `yacc -v` or equivalent to generate a verbose output file
   - Analyze the conflicts report to ensure all identified conflicts are resolved

3. Test the parser with a comprehensive suite of test cases:
   - Valid programs that exercise all grammar rules
   - Edge cases that previously triggered conflicts
   - Programs that use complex language constructs
   - Programs that use Goo-specific extensions

4. Regression testing:
   - Verify that all programs that parsed correctly before still parse correctly
   - Ensure that the AST structure remains consistent for unchanged language constructs
   - Confirm that error reporting still works correctly

5. Performance testing:
   - Measure parsing time before and after changes to ensure no performance regression
   - Check memory usage during parsing to verify no increased resource consumption

6. Code review:
   - Have another team member review the grammar changes for correctness
   - Ensure all changes are well-documented and follow project conventions
